metadata.name: tablepyxl
metadata.summary: Generate Excel documents from html tables

modules.name: tablepyxl.tablepyxl
documentation: Do imports like python3 so our package works for 2 and 3

functions.name: tablepyxl.tablepyxl.write_rows
documentation:
Writes every tr child element of elem to a row in the worksheet

returns the next row after all rows are written

functions.name: tablepyxl.tablepyxl.table_to_sheet
documentation:
Takes a table and workbook and writes the table to a new sheet.
The sheet title will be the same as the table attribute name.

functions.name: tablepyxl.tablepyxl.document_to_workbook
documentation:
Takes a string representation of an html document and writes one sheet for
every table in the document.

The workbook is returned

functions.name: tablepyxl.tablepyxl.document_to_xl
documentation:
Takes a string representation of an html document and writes one sheet for
every table in the document. The workbook is written out to a file called filename

functions.name: tablepyxl.tablepyxl.insert_table_at_cell
documentation: Inserts a table at the location of an openpyxl Cell object.

modules.name: tablepyxl.style
documentation: This is where we handle translating css styles into openpyxl styles
and cascading those from parent to child in the dom.

functions.name: tablepyxl.style.colormap
documentation: Convenience for looking up known colors

functions.name: tablepyxl.style.style_string_to_dict
documentation: Convert css style string to a python dictionary

functions.name: tablepyxl.style.style_dict_to_named_style
documentation: Change css style (stored in a python dictionary) to openpyxl NamedStyle

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.StyleDict

methods.name: tablepyxl.style.StyleDict._keys
documentation: Yielding the keys avoids creating unnecessary data structures
and happily works with both python2 and python3 where the
.keys() method is a dictionary_view in python3 and a list in python2.

methods.name: tablepyxl.style.StyleDict.get_color
documentation: Strip leading # off colors if necessary
It's like a dictionary, but it looks for items in the parent dictionary

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.Element

methods.name: tablepyxl.style.Element.style
documentation: Turn the css styles for this element into an openpyxl NamedStyle.

methods.name: tablepyxl.style.Element.get_dimension
documentation: Extracts the dimension from the style dict of the Element and returns it as a float.

Our base class for representing an html element along with a cascading style.
The element is created along with a parent so that the StyleDict that we store
can point to the parent's StyleDict.

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.Table

methods.name: tablepyxl.style.Table.__init__
documentation: takes an html table object (from lxml)

The concrete implementations of Elements are semantically named for the types of elements we are interested in.
This defines a very concrete tree structure for html tables that we expect to deal with. I prefer this compared to
allowing Element to have an arbitrary number of children and dealing with an abstract element tree.

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.TableHead
documentation: This class maps to the `<th>` element of the html table.

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.TableBody
documentation: This class maps to the `<tbody>` element of the html table.

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.TableRow
documentation: This class maps to the `<tr>` element of the html table.

classes.name: tablepyxl.style.TableCell
documentation: This class maps to the `<td>` element of the html table.
